questions.question: Who were the ancestors of the Mughals on their mother's and father's sides?
questions.explanation: They were descendants of Genghis Khan, ruler of Mongol tribes, and Timur, the ruler of Iran, Iraq, and modern-day Turkey.

questions.question: At what age did Babur succeed to the throne of Ferghana?
questions.explanation: He succeeded in 1494 but was forced to leave his ancestral throne due to the invasion of another Mongol group, the Uzbegs.

questions.question: What was the 'Mansabdari' system?
questions.correct_answer: A grading system used by the Mughals to fix rank, salary, and military responsibilities
questions.explanation: The term 'mansabdar' refers to an individual who holds a 'mansab', meaning a position or rank.

questions.question: Define 'Zat' in the Mughal administrative context.
questions.correct_answer: A numerical value that determined the rank and salary of a mansabdar
questions.explanation: The higher the zat, the more prestigious was the noble’s position in court and the larger his salary.

questions.question: What were 'Jagirs'?
questions.correct_answer: Land assignments given to mansabdars to collect revenue as salary
questions.explanation: Mansabdars did not actually reside in or administer their jagirs; they only had rights to the revenue collected by their servants.

questions.question: What was 'Zabt'?
questions.correct_answer: A revenue system based on careful survey of crop yields and prices over a 10-year period
questions.explanation: It was prevalent in areas where Mughal administrators could survey the land and keep very careful accounts.

questions.question: Who was Akbar's revenue minister who carried out the land surveys?
questions.explanation: He carried out a careful survey of crop yields, prices, and areas cultivated for a 10-year period (1570–1580).

questions.question: What is the meaning of 'Sulh-i-kul'?
questions.explanation: It was a principle of governance based on ethics, honesty, and justice that did not discriminate between people of different religions.

questions.question: Who wrote the 'Akbar Nama' and 'Ain-i Akbari'?
questions.explanation: He was a close friend and courtier of Akbar; the Ain-i Akbari deals with Akbar’s administration, household, and army.

questions.question: What were 'Zamindars' in the Mughal empire?
questions.correct_answer: Intermediaries who collected land revenue from peasants
questions.explanation: The Mughals used one term—zamindars—to describe all intermediaries, whether they were local headmen or powerful chieftains.

notes.title: Primogeniture vs. Coparcenary
notes.content: The Mughals did not follow primogeniture (eldest son inherits); they followed the Timurid custom of coparcenary inheritance, or a division of the inheritance amongst all the sons.

notes.title: Ibadat Khana
notes.content: A prayer house where Akbar held discussions with learned individuals from various religions like Ulama, Brahmanas, and Jesuit priests.

notes.title: The Big Six
notes.content: The major Mughal emperors were Babur, Humayun, Akbar, Jahangir, Shah Jahan, and Aurangzeb.

facts.fact: Cannons were an important addition to 16th-century warfare; Babur used them effectively in the first battle of Panipat.
Nur Jahan (Mehrunnisa) exercised tremendous influence in Jahangir's court and had coins struck in her name.
The Taj Mahal was constructed during the reign of Shah Jahan as a mausoleum for his wife Mumtaz Mahal.
